content.problemStatement:
Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding a specific target value within a sorted array or list. It basically uses the ordered nature of the array to its advantage to find an element in an array very fast.

Binary search works as -


Initialise the search space with two indices, leftleftleft and rightrightright. Initially, left is set to the beginning of the array and right is set to the end.
Take the mid-point of the search space as mid=(left+right)/2mid = (left + right)/2mid=(left+right)/2.
Compare the target element to the element at the midmidmid index.

If the target value matches the midpoint value, the search is successful.
If the target value is less than the midpoint value, update right=mid−1right = mid - 1right=mid−1 to search the left half.
If the target value is greater than the midpoint value, update left=mid+1left = mid + 1left=mid+1 to search the right half.


Repeat till the search space is not empty, i.e, right>=leftright >= leftright>=left.
TaskThe binary search function searches for the index of the element, returns back the index (1-base) if the element is found, else returns -1.
Complete the implementation of the binary search function to solve this problem.
constraints: Array elements are integers and sorted in non‑decreasing order
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a line with two integers N and X, where N is the size of the sorted array and X is the target value. The next line contains N space‑separated integers in non‑decreasing order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the 1‑based index of X in the array if it exists, otherwise -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case the target 7 is at position 3 (1‑based). In the second test case 6 does not appear, so -1 is printed.

solution.approach: For each test case we perform a classic binary search on the sorted array. We keep two pointers, left (initially 0) and right (initially N‑1). While left <= right we compute mid = (left + right) / 2 (integer division). If arr[mid] equals the target we return mid+1 (1‑based index). If arr[mid] is larger we move right to mid‑1, otherwise we move left to mid+1. If the loop finishes without finding the target we return -1. The algorithm runs in O(log N) time per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log N) per test case, O(T log N) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) auxiliary space besides the input array.
